article_meta.title: Supreme Court ruling against Trump’s tariffs is unlikely to mean an end to trade policy chaos 1 of 3 | Containers are st

reasoning: The summary maintains neutral language, avoids the article’s loaded terms (e.g., “stunning rebuke,” “chaotic,” “strong-armed,” “cudgel,” “mess”), and attributes interpretations to sources. It balances perspectives and focuses on factual provisions and implications without adopting the source’s rhetoric.

custom_reasoning.spin: Uses neutral verbs like “invalidated,” “exploring,” “complicates,” and avoids charged phrasing from the article.
custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: All claims are either directly supported by the article or framed as expert/analyst expectations.
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: Evaluative statements are attributed or carefully hedged (e.g., ‘expected,’ ‘may face’).
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: No alarming or emotive language; avoids the article’s dramatic terms.
custom_reasoning.mudslinging: No personal attacks or character judgments.
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No attributions of motives or intentions without sourcing.
custom_reasoning.slant: Covers legal options, international reactions, and refund issues proportionally.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Includes major angles: SCOTUS decision, Sections 122/301, EU/UK deals, refunds, and business uncertainty.
custom_reasoning.word_choice: Avoids politically loaded terms; uses technical, neutral vocabulary.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Tone matches the article’s focus on uncertainty without amplification.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: Treats government officials, analysts, and lawyers as sources without privileging one group.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: Substantially more neutral than the source’s headline and rhetoric.
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: Represents key perspectives (U.S. officials, EU officials, legal experts, analysts) with proportional weight.
